response.justification: Sarah Smith has a broad software background and genuine geospatial experience through GISCollective and Green Map, which are relevant to the domain. However, the role's core technical requirement is modern C++ (or Rust) for high-performance routing data pipelines, and her C++ experience is both minimal and dated (a six-month stint at National Instruments in 2014–2015 plus a bachelor's thesis project). Her career trajectory has been firmly in the TypeScript/Node.js/web space for the past decade, with no evidence of routing tile generation, navigation pipeline work, or Rust. The geospatial angle is appealing but insufficient to offset the deep C++ systems gap.
strengths: Hands-on geospatial/GIS domain experience via GISCollective open-source platform and Green Map applications, directly adjacent to the job's geospatial focus
Demonstrated high-performance, low-latency distributed systems work at Dunnhumby (millions of requests/sec, async I/O, fiber-based architectures)
Strong software craftsmanship culture (TDD, CI/CD, trunk-based development) that aligns with the role's quality-assurance and reliability expectations
concerns: C++ experience is minimal and over a decade old; no Rust experience at all — the role's primary language requirement is essentially unmet
No experience with routing tile pipelines, navigation guidance systems, or map data transformation, which are the core deliverables of the position
Career has moved consistently away from systems programming toward web/TypeScript/cloud, making a pivot to C++ infrastructure work a high-risk hire
